metadata.summary: Production deployment of OpenAI fine-tuned models: pipeline, job manager, cost estimation, monitoring, gradual rollout, model lifecycle, and agent-specific gotchas.

content.title: OpenAI Fine-Tuned Model Production Deployment
content.summary: Deploying a fine-tuned OpenAI model to production requires: a production pipeline with logging and error handling, gradual rollout (10% → 50% → 100%), inference cost monitoring, model ID management in version control, and agent-specific patterns for asynchronous polling and event parsing.

why.p: Fine-tuned model inference costs are 2-3x the base model at equivalent quality for some tasks. Gradual rollout prevents a degraded model from affecting 100% of users. The model lifecycle requires tracking: model IDs change on every retrain, training data hashes must be versioned alongside model IDs, and deprecated models must be deleted to avoid storage costs.

Agentic workflows introduce additional failure modes: fine-tuning jobs run 15-120 minutes so the agent must poll asynchronously. A failed job still returns 200 OK on creation, so status checks must inspect job.status, not HTTP status. The model ID must be written to a config file, never hardcoded.

list.item: Fine-tuned model has passed quality gates (accuracy, format compliance, human preference rate).
Managing multiple fine-tuning jobs in a pipeline (list, cancel, monitor, delete).
Integrating fine-tuning into an agentic SDD workflow with async polling.
Planning model lifecycle: versioning, retraining triggers, storage cleanup.
Model has not passed evaluation quality gates — do not deploy a model that has not been benchmarked against the base model.
Experimental or exploratory fine-tuning — use ephemeral jobs, do not productionize until requirements are stable.

section.name: production-pipeline
section.p: The FineTuningPipeline class encapsulates the full SFT workflow with logging, error handling, train/validation splitting, and async job monitoring.

example.title: FineTuningPipeline — production class

section.name: job-manager
section.p: The FineTuneJobManager provides list, status, cancel, and model deletion operations for managing multiple fine-tuning jobs.

example.title: FineTuneJobManager — job lifecycle management

section.name: cli-tool
section.p: CLI for fine-tuning operations (upload, train, status, list, cancel, evaluate):

example.title: Fine-tuning CLI skeleton

list.item: Pre-production: test with production-like traffic, verify error handling, confirm rate limits sufficient, set up monitoring/logging.
Staging rollout: deploy to staging environment, run smoke tests with representative queries.
Gradual rollout: 10% → 50% → 100% with monitoring at each stage. Roll back on error rate increase or quality regression.
Post-launch: monitor inference costs, track quality metrics over time, collect user feedback, plan retraining if quality degrades.
Version every model: document fine-tuned model ID + training data hash + hyperparameters + eval results in version control alongside application config.
Retraining triggers: quality degradation (eval score drops), distribution shift (new user query patterns), new training data availability.
Model deletion: delete superseded models via client.models.delete() to avoid storage costs. Retain only the current production model and one previous version for rollback.
Model ID format: ft:gpt-4.1-mini-2025-01-01:org::xxxxx — store in config files, never hardcode in application code.
Fine-tuning jobs run 15-120 minutes — agent must poll asynchronously (60s interval), not block a synchronous thread.
A failed job still returns 200 OK on creation — always check job.status, not HTTP status code.
client.fine_tuning.jobs.list_events(job_id) is the only way to see training progress — parse these for loss values.
Human checkpoint before routing live traffic is mandatory — automated eval on synthetic data is insufficient.
Do not share training data containing PII with OpenAI unless a Data Processing Agreement is in place and users have consented.
Enable data sharing (integrations param) for inference discounts only if training data is non-sensitive.

section.name: services-and-tools
section.p: Services and tools for fine-tuning workflows:

list.item: openai Python SDK — jobs, file upload, model listing. Install: pip install openai.
tiktoken — count tokens in training file before upload. Install: pip install tiktoken.
jsonlines — read/write JSONL files. Install: pip install jsonlines.
openai-evals — eval harness for comparing base vs fine-tuned. Install: pip install evals (github.com/openai/evals).
promptfoo — A/B eval fine-tuned vs base. Install: npx promptfoo (promptfoo.dev).
Weights and Biases — log eval metrics, track model versions, agent reads via API. SaaS.
Braintrust — eval and tracing for OpenAI models, agent-friendly REST API. SaaS.
Langfuse — observability, compare base vs fine-tuned via traces. OSS/SaaS.

antipattern.description: Hardcoding the fine-tuned model ID in application source code.
antipattern.reason: Model IDs change on every retrain. Hardcoded IDs require a code deploy to update, coupling the retraining pipeline to the application release cycle. Store model IDs in config files or environment variables, updated by the pipeline on successful completion.

antipattern.description: Routing 100% of production traffic immediately to a new fine-tuned model.
antipattern.reason: Even a model that passes eval can fail on real production traffic due to distribution differences. Gradual rollout (10% → 50% → 100%) limits blast radius and provides real traffic signals for quality monitoring before full deployment.

antipattern.description: Not tracking which training data produced which model version.
antipattern.reason: Without version tracking, debugging quality regressions is impossible. When a model degrades, you need to know what changed: training data, hyperparameters, or base model version. Document model ID + training data hash + eval results in version control on every retrain.
